mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Austria on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, right in the centre. Austria is landlocked, surrounded by Germany, the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Italy, Switzerland and Liechtenstein 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Austria?
mapQuestions.answer: Vienna, on the Danube River. About 1.9 million people. One of the most "liveable" cities in the world year after year.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Austria speak?
mapQuestions.answer: German, in an Austrian variety that has its own words and accents. Austrians often joke that they speak "German with a smile". Most Austrians also speak good English.

mapQuestions.question: How much of Austria is mountain?
mapQuestions.answer: Nearly two-thirds. The Alps cover most of western and southern Austria. The country has 13 peaks over 3,000 m, the highest being Grossglockner at 3,798 m.

mapQuestions.question: How big was the Habsburg Empire?
mapQuestions.answer: For about 600 years (1276 to 1918), Austria was the heart of the Habsburg Empire, which at its peak ruled much of Central Europe, parts of Italy, Spain, the Netherlands, and bits of the Americas.

foods.name: Wiener Schnitzel
foods.description: A thin slice of veal pounded flat, breaded, deep-fried in butter until crisp and golden, served with a lemon wedge and lingonberry jam. Vienna's most famous dish.

foods.name: Brezel + Käsekrainer
foods.description: Salted soft pretzels and Austrian cheese-stuffed sausages, eaten standing up at a "Würstelstand" (sausage stand) in any Austrian city.

foods.name: Sachertorte
foods.description: A dense dark chocolate cake with a thin layer of apricot jam, coated in chocolate icing. Invented in Vienna in 1832 by Franz Sacher. Served with unsweetened whipped cream.

foods.name: Kaiserschmarrn
foods.description: A fluffy shredded pancake torn into pieces, dusted with powdered sugar, served with stewed plums or apple sauce. The Habsburg emperor's favourite dessert.

foods.name: Käsespätzle
foods.description: "Cheese pasta": small egg-pasta dumplings layered with grated mountain cheese and topped with crispy fried onions. The Austrian Alps comfort food.

foods.name: Apfelstrudel
foods.description: A long, thin, rolled pastry with sliced apples, raisins, cinnamon, sugar and breadcrumbs inside. Served warm with vanilla sauce or whipped cream.

scavengerItems.label: Schönbrunn or Hofburg Palace in Vienna
A bright-yellow Vienna tram on the Ring road
A street musician or chamber orchestra playing classical music
St. Stephen's Cathedral with its patterned tile roof
Snow-covered Alps with a wooden chalet on a hillside
A cable car running up to a ski village (Innsbruck, Kitzbühel)
A Lipizzaner horse at the Spanish Riding School in Vienna
A "Heuriger" wine tavern outside Vienna with grape leaves over the door
A baroque onion-domed church on a hilltop
Mozart's birthplace (the yellow house) in Salzburg
The actual front door from "The Sound of Music" filming in Salzburg
Hallstatt salt mines (the OLDEST salt mines in the world, in use for 7,000 years)

geoMatches.description: The capital. The old imperial city of the Habsburgs. Palaces (Schönbrunn, Hofburg), the Vienna State Opera, hundreds of coffee houses, classical music in every direction.
The "city of Mozart", in the foothills of the Alps. Birthplace of Mozart, filming location of The Sound of Music, baroque Old Town, big music festival every summer.
A tiny chocolate-box lakeside village in the Salzkammergut region. So photogenic that a Chinese property developer built an EXACT replica of it in China.
The Alpine capital of Tyrol, ringed by 2,500 m mountains. Skiing in winter, hiking in summer, with a Habsburg-era old town in the centre.
A wine-growing UNESCO valley along the Danube River, with hilltop monasteries, river castles, and steep terraced vineyards.
Austria's highest peak (3,798 m) and the spectacular Grossglockner High Alpine Road, a 48 km mountain road with 36 hairpin bends climbing to 2,571 m.

senses.smell: Coffee and warm pastries in a Vienna café, pine forests in the Alps, woodsmoke from chalets, fresh-baked pretzels at a Würstelstand, cold Salzburg fountains in summer…
senses.hear: Classical music drifting from cafés, "Grüß Gott!" everywhere, Vienna trams ringing their bells, alpine cowbells in summer meadows, opera echoing from the Vienna State Opera house…
senses.taste: Crispy schnitzel with lemon, dense chocolate Sachertorte, fluffy Kaiserschmarrn pancakes, sharp Austrian mountain cheese, fresh white wine from the Wachau Valley…
senses.feel: The cold of an alpine lake on a summer day, the warmth of a coffee house in winter, smooth marble floors of a Habsburg palace, the bumpy cobblestones of the Salzburg Old Town, the soft fur of a Lipizzaner horse at the Spanish Riding School…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Who was Mozart?
stories.body: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born on 27 January 1756, in a yellow house on Getreidegasse 9 in Salzburg, Austria. He started composing at age 5. He wrote his first opera at 11. By the time he died at just 35, he had written over 600 pieces of music, including operas like "The Magic Flute" and "Don Giovanni" that are still performed today. Mozart is universally considered one of the greatest composers in the history of Western music. Salzburg has been a "Mozart pilgrimage" city for over 200 years, with his birthplace as a museum, statues, music festivals, and even Mozart-shaped chocolates ("Mozartkugel"). His music shaped the way classical music sounds.

stories.question: Why is Vienna obsessed with coffee?
stories.body: When the Turks besieged Vienna in 1683 and were eventually defeated, they left behind bags of strange dark beans. Local merchants experimented with them, and the first Vienna coffee house opened soon after. Within decades, Vienna had developed a unique "coffee house" culture: ornate cafés where you order one cup of coffee and stay for hours, reading newspapers (hung on long wooden poles), playing chess, writing books, gossiping. UNESCO put Viennese coffee-house culture on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2011. Famous Vienna coffee houses (Café Central, Café Sacher, Café Hawelka) have served writers, scientists and revolutionaries for over 150 years. Sigmund Freud wrote there. So did Stefan Zweig. So did Leon Trotsky.

stories.question: Why is one tiny village famous worldwide?
stories.body: Hallstatt is a village of fewer than 800 people on a deep alpine lake in central Austria. Half-timbered houses, a church spire, snow-capped peaks behind it, all reflected perfectly in the lake. It is one of the most photographed views in Europe. In 2012 a Chinese company built an EXACT, full-size replica of Hallstatt in Guangdong province, copying the houses, the lake, the church. The villagers were not asked permission. Real Hallstatt now gets so many tourists (sometimes over a million a year) that the local government has tried to limit them. Still, sunrise over Hallstatt is unforgettable.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole village was copied brick-by-brick into another country. Who really "owns" a place like that?

stories.question: Who were the Habsburgs?
stories.body: For around 600 years (1276 to 1918), Austria was ruled by the Habsburg family, one of the most powerful royal dynasties in European history. At their peak, the Habsburgs ruled Austria, Hungary, Bohemia, parts of Italy and the Netherlands, Spain, the Spanish Americas, and the Holy Roman Empire. They expanded mostly through strategic marriages instead of war. Vienna was their capital. The Hofburg Palace (their winter residence), Schönbrunn Palace (their summer residence), the State Opera, the museums, the Spanish Riding School, all date from the Habsburg era. The empire ended after World War I in 1918, when Austria became a much smaller republic.

stories.question: How old is Austria's salt mining?
stories.body: In the mountains around Hallstatt are some of the OLDEST salt mines in the world, still in use today. People have been mining salt here for over 7,000 years. Salt was essential for preserving food in the days before refrigeration, and Austria's ability to mine and trade salt was one reason it grew rich. (The word "salary" comes from the Latin for "salt money".) The Hallstatt mines have been worked continuously since the Bronze Age. Modern tunnels run for kilometres into the mountain, sometimes connecting to ancient Bronze Age galleries with prehistoric wooden mining tools still preserved by the salt. You can tour the mines today.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Austria so far?
Have you tried "Grüß Gott" or "danke" yet?
Vienna, Salzburg, Alps, lake, which has felt the most "Austria"?
Did you eat a real Sachertorte? Was it as chocolatey as everyone says?
How does Austrian classical music feel different from modern music to you?
For 600 years, this small country was the centre of an empire that stretched from Spain to Hungary. How does that history feel in modern Vienna?

wordSearch.hint: Austrian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown Alpine cow
animals.description: Wearing big bells, grazing on summer pastures across the Tyrolean Alps. The bells help farmers find them.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In Austrian alpine forests, especially common in the Hohe Tauern National Park.

animals.name: Lipizzaner horse
animals.description: The famous white show horses of the Spanish Riding School in Vienna. Bred since 1580.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: Nests in the Austrian Alps, especially in Hohe Tauern. About 350 pairs.

animals.name: Marmot
animals.description: Fat, fluffy alpine rodents that whistle loudly when scared. Common in high meadows in summer.

animals.name: Alpine ibex
animals.description: A wild mountain goat with huge curved horns, reintroduced to the high Austrian Alps. Around 1,000 in the country.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Returning slowly to the Austrian Alps after long absence. A handful of packs documented.
